title: PM Models to Know
summary: The thinking models PMBOK 8 references, grouped by theme so you can name the family first, then pick the specific model. PMBOK 8 calls out commonly used models for leadership, motivation, conflict, change, communication, complexity, negotiation and planning - this guide catalogues them for quick recognition.

sections.heading: What a model is - and how PMBOK 8 uses them
sections.body:
PMBOK 8 defines a model as a thinking strategy: a simplified way to explain a process or phenomenon so you can reason about it and choose a response. You are not asked to apply every model in depth, but to recognise the right lens for a situation.

PMBOK 8 explicitly lists commonly used models by theme - situational leadership, communication, motivation, change, complexity, conflict, negotiation, planning, process groups and stakeholder salience. This guide catalogues them under those themes. Models with their own fuller guide (Tuckman, Thomas-Kilmann, motivation theories, salience) are listed at the end.

blocks.title: Exam mantra
lines: Match the model to the context; do not force one model onto every problem.
First name the family (complexity, change, leadership); then pick the specific model.
Models, methods and artifacts are chosen (tailored), not mandated.

sections.heading: Situational leadership and coaching models
sections.body: PMBOK 8 groups these as situational leadership models: match the style to the person and the task. There is no single best style.

blocks.title: Situational Leadership II - style by follower development (competence x commitment)

rows.value: Low competence, high commitment - tell and show.
Some competence, low commitment - explain and persuade.
High competence, variable commitment - encourage and share decisions.
High competence, high commitment - hand over responsibility.

blocks.title: Coaching conversation frames -> what it says

rows.value: Goal, Reality (current state), Options, Will (way forward and commitment). A single coaching conversation.
Outcome, Situation, Choices, Actions, Review. Longer-term development with built-in follow-up.

blocks.title: Cue
blocks.text: There is no single best leadership style - the answer is the one matched to the follower's development level and the task.

sections.heading: Change models: the people side
sections.body: PMBOK 8 references change models for how people and organisations move through change - not the technical rollout. A favourite exam axis is the LEVEL each one operates at: the whole organisation/system, or one individual.

blocks.title: Organisation / system level -> what it says

rows.value: Unfreeze (loosen the status quo), change (move to the new way), refreeze (embed it). The simplest frame.
Top-led, org-wide sequence: create urgency, build coalition and vision, enable action and short-term wins, anchor change in the culture.
Late status quo -> resistance -> chaos (performance dips) -> integration -> new status quo. Tracks a GROUP's performance and normalises the temporary dip.
The adoption sequence each PERSON passes through: Awareness, Desire, Knowledge, Ability, Reinforcement.
Separates external change from each person's internal transition: Ending (letting go), Neutral Zone (messy in-between), New Beginning. People transition slower than systems change.
Emotional stages a person moves through: denial, anger, bargaining, depression, acceptance. Reads resistance and grief during change.

blocks.title: Cue
lines: Level: INDIVIDUAL = ADKAR, Bridges, Kubler-Ross (one person at a time).
Process vs experience: Lewin/Kotter describe the change PROCESS; Bridges/Satir/Kubler-Ross describe the human EXPERIENCE.
People lag the system change - plan for the dip.

sections.heading: Complexity models: Cynefin and Stacey
sections.body: PMBOK 8 references complexity models to decide how much rigour and how iterative your approach should be. Cynefin sorts a situation into domains; each calls for a different response.

figure.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 320 205' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' fill='none'><rect x='20' y='16' width='140' height='88' fill='#2F6F5E' fill-opacity='0.13' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='0.8'/><rect x='160' y='16' width='140' height='88' fill='#1F5FA8' fill-opacity='0.13' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='0.8'/><rect x='20' y='104' width='140' height='88' fill='#B5462F' fill-opacity='0.13' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='0.8'/><rect x='160' y='104' width='140' height='88' fill='#C98A1F' fill-opacity='0.13' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='0.8'/><text x='90' y='50' fill='currentColor' font-size='11' text-anchor='middle'>Complex</text><text x='90' y='66' fill='currentColor' font-size='8' text-anchor='middle'>probe-sense-respond</text><text x='230' y='50' fill='currentColor' font-size='11' text-anchor='middle'>Complicated</text><text x='230' y='66' fill='currentColor' font-size='8' text-anchor='middle'>sense-analyse-respond</text><text x='90' y='142' fill='currentColor' font-size='11' text-anchor='middle'>Chaotic</text><text x='90' y='158' fill='currentColor' font-size='8' text-anchor='middle'>act-sense-respond</text><text x='230' y='142' fill='currentColor' font-size='11' text-anchor='middle'>Clear</text><text x='230' y='158' fill='currentColor' font-size='8' text-anchor='middle'>sense-categorise-respond</text><circle cx='160' cy='104' r='23' fill='none' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1'/><text x='160' y='107' fill='currentColor' font-size='8' text-anchor='middle'>Disorder</text></svg>
figure.caption: Cynefin: match the response to the kind of problem; Disorder is not yet knowing which domain you are in.

rows.value: Cause and effect are plain. Sense-categorise-respond; apply best practice.
Knowable but needs expertise. Sense-analyse-respond; apply good practice.
Cause and effect only clear in hindsight. Probe-sense-respond; let practice emerge via safe-to-fail experiments.
No clear cause and effect; act first. Act-sense-respond to establish order.
The centre - you do not yet know which domain you are in.

blocks.title: Stacey matrix - agreement x certainty -> what it says

rows.value: Agreement on requirements and certainty on approach - simple; suggests predictive delivery.
Complicated or complex; favours adaptive or hybrid approaches.
Low agreement and low certainty - tends toward chaos.

blocks.title: Cue
blocks.text: Complex and adaptive go together: high uncertainty or low agreement points to iterative, experiment-led delivery.

sections.heading: Planning and team-performance models
sections.body: PMBOK 8 references planning models that capture the cost trade-off in planning effort, and team models that describe how a team matures.

rows.value: Too little planning = costly rework and missed risks; too much = wasted effort and delay. The sweet spot minimises total cost and shifts with how predictable the work is.

Seven stages a team moves through, from orientation and trust building (creating the team) to implementation, high performance and renewal (sustaining the team). Complements Tuckman by adding the resolving question at each stage.

blocks.title: Cue
blocks.text: On highly uncertain work, plan less up front and re-plan often - the sweet spot moves left.

sections.heading: Motivation, conflict, communication and other model families
sections.body: PMBOK 8 also references motivation, conflict, communication, negotiation and salience models. These are covered in depth in their own guides; here are the family pointers so the catalogue is complete.

blocks.title: Family -> models (see the dedicated guide for depth)

rows.value: Tuckman - forming, storming, norming, performing, adjourning.
Thomas-Kilmann - competing, collaborating, compromising, avoiding, accommodating.
Maslow, Herzberg, McGregor X/Y, McClelland, Vroom and others.
Sender-receiver / communication models - channels, encoding, noise, feedback.
Salience and Power-Interest - prioritising stakeholders.
Reaching and recording a decision.

blocks.title: Exam tip
lines: In the exam, first name the family (complexity, change, leadership), then pick the specific model.
Use the dedicated guides for depth; use this one to recognise the leadership, change, complexity and planning models at a glance.

source: PM Cram study notes - reworded for the current ECO-based PMP exam and framed around the commonly used models PMBOK 8 references.
